role_summary: Cynthia Baker Anderson and Fletcher Anderson (b. 1938, Bogalusa, Louisiana): leaders of the Bogalusa civil rights movement; Fletcher Anderson was a founding member (1965) of the Deacons of Defense and Justice in Washington Parish, the Bogalusa-based armed-self-defense organization that protected NAACP demonstrations and the homes of local civil rights leaders; the Andersons were also active in the Bogalusa Voters League and the federal job-discrimination litigation against the Crown Zellerbach paper mill.
ai_reading: The Andersons' top semantic neighbor is the joint Geraldine Crawford Bennett / Toni Breaux / Willie Elliot Jenkins interview (entry 46) at a cosine count of 158; the broader cluster includes Richard Barry Sobol (entry 104, the LCDC Louisiana attorney whose Bogalusa work overlapped with the Andersons'), Charles McLaurin (entry 17), and Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96). The Sobol pairing is the catalog's clearest direct-overlap Louisiana cosine: Sobol's 1960s LCDC litigation in Louisiana included Bogalusa cases the Andersons participated in. The catalog flags a -0.85 reading on the Nonviolence as Theology vs Armed Self-Defense axis (far toward Nonviolence as Theology) as the most-distinctive structural reading. For a founding member of an armed-self-defense organization (the Deacons of Defense), the embedding's pull toward the Nonviolence-as-Theology pole is the catalog's clearest case of the Deacons-of-Defense-related AI-vs-culture mismatch, paralleling the BPP-cohort inversion (Aaron Dixon -0.73, Kathleen Cleaver -0.80, Elmer Dixon -1.00); the embedding catches the theological-defensive register of the Bogalusa community-protection vocabulary over the cultural surface-armed-self-defense framing.
biographical_paragraph: While the Andersons' LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), their external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project's published interview summary [src: 1, src: 2]. Fletcher Anderson was born in 1938 in Bogalusa, Louisiana; he graduated from Central Memorial High School and worked at the Crown Zellerbach paper mill in a series of positions, eventually as an overhead crane operator. He and Cynthia Baker married and had three children. In 1965 Fletcher joined other African American men in Washington Parish to form a Deacons of Defense and Justice chapter, the Bogalusa-anchored armed-civilian-defense organization that protected NAACP demonstrations and the homes of local civil rights leaders during the height of segregationist violence in the region; he became one of the chapter's leaders [src: 2]. The Andersons were also active in the Bogalusa Voters League. They participated in the federal job-discrimination litigation against the Crown Zellerbach paper mill that ran across the 1960s and 1970s. Their National Museum of African American History and Culture oral history record documents their organizing work and the contemporary state of race relations in Bogalusa [src: 2].
